channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: NASA's Roman Space Telescope arrives in Florida ahead of SpaceX Falcon Heavy launch this summer - Space
item.description:
1. NASA's Roman Space Telescope arrives in Florida ahead of SpaceX Falcon Heavy launch this summer  Space
  2. NASA’s Next Generation Telescope Arrives in Florida Ahead of Launch  NASA Science (.gov)
  3. NASA's Nancy Grace Roman telescope arrives at Kennedy Space Center  Florida Today
  4. NASA’s Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope arrives in Florida  Spaceflight Now
  5. NASA’s $4.3 billion planet-hunting Nancy Grace Roman Space telescope arrives in Florida  Orlando Sentinel

item.title: With Starfall, SpaceX eyes an edge in global cargo delivery from orbit - Ars Technica
item.description:
1. With Starfall, SpaceX eyes an edge in global cargo delivery from orbit  Ars Technica
  2. SpaceX launching its 1st 'Starfall' reentry capsule early on June 23: Watch it live  Space
  3. Live coverage: SpaceX to launch reentry capsule demo mission called ‘Starfall’  Spaceflight Now
  4. SpaceX is launching a secret spacecraft that could change how things are made in space  Teslarati
  5. SpaceX is set to fly its first Starfall capsule Tuesday, a reentry vehicle built to bring up to 1,000 kilograms of cargo back from orbit  Space Daily

item.title: NASA’s Webb Finds Clues to Ancient, Distant Origin of Comet 3I/ATLAS - NASA Science (.gov)
item.description:
1. NASA’s Webb Finds Clues to Ancient, Distant Origin of Comet 3I/ATLAS  NASA Science (.gov)
  2. Interstellar comet may be oldest object seen in our solar system, scientists say  The Guardian
  3. Webb finds clues to ancient origin of Comet 3I/ATLAS  European Space Agency
  4. The Chemistry of Interstellar Comet 3I/ATLAS Is Unlike Anything We've Ever Seen  ScienceAlert
  5. Interstellar Comet 3I/ATLAS is almost as old as the universe itself  Scientific American

item.title: June Bootids meteor shower to peak this week - ABC News - Breaking News, Latest News and Videos
item.description:
1. June Bootids meteor shower to peak this week  ABC News - Breaking News, Latest News and Videos
  2. Meteor Activity Outlook for June 20-26, 2026  American Meteor Society
  3. The most unpredictable meteor shower of the year peaks this week. Here's what to expect  Space
  4. The June Bootids have flared to dozens of meteors an hour in rare years like 1998, but most years the shower is so weak it shows almost nothing — and no outburst is forecast for 2026  Space Daily
  5. June Bootids 2026: Complete guide to peak timing, visibility, what to expect  Geo News
item.source: ABC News - Breaking News, Latest News and Videos

item.title: For a decade the Pink Planet was too faint for any ground telescope to study — and when James Webb finally caught its spectrum, the best fit to the light was an atmosphere hazed with salt clouds - Space Daily
item.description:
1. For a decade the Pink Planet was too faint for any ground telescope to study — and when James Webb finally caught its spectrum, the best fit to the light was an atmosphere hazed with salt clouds  Space Daily
  2. "Pink Planet" is surrounded by salty clouds, researchers using Webb telescope find  CBS News
  3. James Webb Space Telescope finds a salty surprise on famous 'Pink Planet'  Space
  4. Webb telescope finds possible salt clouds on a pink alien world  Mashable
  5. Scientists Discover Salty Clouds On the Mysterious Pink Planet  Yahoo

item.title: Our sun is destined to 'kick and spit' its way across the solar system when it dies - Space
item.description:
1. Our sun is destined to 'kick and spit' its way across the solar system when it dies  Space
  2. The mystery of a dying star's final kick  Courthouse News
  3. A Star's Death Throes Involves a Lot of Kicking  Caltech
  4. A star's death throes involve a lot of kicking  Phys.org

item.title: A space telescope that has spent 22 years detecting the most powerful explosions in the universe is now falling toward Earth, and a startup in Arizona built its rescue craft in seven months flat — a schedule Science described as almost unheard of for a NASA m - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A space telescope that has spent 22 years detecting the most powerful explosions in the universe is now falling toward Earth, and a startup in Arizona built its rescue craft in seven months flat — a schedule Science described as almost unheard of for a NASA m  Space Daily
  2. 'No one thought it was going to be possible.' A space telescope is falling out of space. This is NASA's daring plan to save it. (video)  Space
  3. Rocket Attached to Aircraft for Katalyst-NASA Swift Boost  NASA Science (.gov)
  4. A bold satellite rescue mission came together in record time, but will it work?  Ars Technica
  5. NASA's Swift Boost Mission Will Launch Later This Month To Rescue A Falling Telescope  Engadget

item.title: Anyone can fake a scientific image with AI, tricking even academic journals – and undermining trust in science - The Conversation
item.description:
1. Anyone can fake a scientific image with AI, tricking even academic journals – and undermining trust in science  The Conversation
  2. AI-generated images undermine scientific visual evidence  Let's Data Science
  3. The Real Threat Isn't Deepfakes—It's the Collapse of Trust": Amitabh Kumar on Building Contrails AI  Indian Startup Times
  4. New chip fights deepfakes with built-in cryptographic signing  Interesting Engineering

item.title: Scientists thought they knew what's lurking at the centre of our Galaxy. But they may have been completely wrong this whole time - BBC Sky at Night Magazine
item.description:
1. Scientists thought they knew what's lurking at the centre of our Galaxy. But they may have been completely wrong this whole time  BBC Sky at Night Magazine
  2. In 2022 the Event Horizon Telescope revealed the first image of the monster at our galaxy's center, Sagittarius A* — a black hole 4 million times the Sun's mass, sitting quietly 27,000 light-years away the whole time  Space Daily

item.title: An unusual strategy helps some tropical butterflies live 25 times longer than their relatives - KSL News
item.description:
1. An unusual strategy helps some tropical butterflies live 25 times longer than their relatives  KSL News
  2. Some tropical butterfly species have unlocked an evolutionary way to live longer  CNN
  3. Evolution of increased longevity and slowed ageing in a genus of tropical butterfly  Nature
  4. Butterfly that barely ages could help unlock longevity secrets  ScienceDaily
  5. The Butterfly Longevity Diet  The Atlantic

item.title: A hidden fungal network longer than the distance to the Sun wraps the roots of 70% of Earth's plants, and the first global map finds it densest not under rainforests but wild grasslands - Earth.com
item.description:
1. A hidden fungal network longer than the distance to the Sun wraps the roots of 70% of Earth's plants, and the first global map finds it densest not under rainforests but wild grasslands  Earth.com
  2. Our Hidden Fungal Networks Could Reach Beyond the Solar System  Mother Jones
  3. There's an Astounding Amount of Fungi Beneath Our Feet  Newser
  4. Nearly All Plants Depend on Tiny Underground Fungi. The Microbes' Vast Global Networks Were Just Mapped for the First Time  Smithsonian Magazine
  5. Scientists just finished mapping the 110-quadrillion-mile network hiding under your feet  Yahoo Creators

item.title: A mysterious gamma-ray stream comes from the Milky Way's center. Could dark matter have something to do wit... - Yahoo
item.description:

item.title: A Dark Dimension Could Link Two of the Universe’s Great Unknowns - Quanta Magazine
item.description:
1. A Dark Dimension Could Link Two of the Universe’s Great Unknowns  Quanta Magazine
  2. Scientists explore a mysterious ‘Magic Line’ that could rewrite the story of the universe  Moneycontrol.com
  3. What is the phantom divide: The universe's mysterious 'magic line' that could change cosmology  The Times of India

item.title: Voyager 1 has been travelling non-stop since 1977, faster than a speeding bullet every second of every day, and it has not yet reached the distance of a single light-year from Earth - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Voyager 1 has been travelling non-stop since 1977, faster than a speeding bullet every second of every day, and it has not yet reached the distance of a single light-year from Earth  Space Daily
  2. It's Official: Voyager 1 Will Break A Cosmic Record, Reaching 1 Light-Day From Earth On November 18, 2026  IFLScience
  3. The 49-Year-Old Spacecraft Still Talking From Deep Space  HackerNoon
  4. A probe is about to enter a realm humanity has only imagined until now  Futura, le média qui explore le monde
  5. Voyager 1 launched in 1977 on a four-year mission and is still flying 49 years later, now so far away that in November 2026 a radio signal will take a full 24 hours to reach it — so when engineers say "good morning" on a Monday, the answer won't arrive until  19FortyFive

item.title: Remember that star that was supposed to explode in 2024? It didn't, but it could still happen soon - CBC

item.title: On March 13, 1989, a geomagnetic storm collapsed Quebec's entire power grid in just 90 seconds — leaving 6 million people in the cold and dark before most of them had even woken up - Space Daily
item.description:
1. On March 13, 1989, a geomagnetic storm collapsed Quebec's entire power grid in just 90 seconds — leaving 6 million people in the cold and dark before most of them had even woken up  Space Daily
  2. How to Protect Earth Against Violent Space Weather  Nautilus | Science
  3. Solar storms don't harm people directly — they induce electric currents in long metal, meaning the power lines, pipelines and undersea internet cables we've strung across the planet are exactly what they attack  Space Daily
  4. GWYNNE DYER: Solar storms: one thing less to worry about?  Windsor Star
  5. Scientists Propose Creating a Space Shield Against Solar Storms  Zamin.uz

item.title: NASA Almost Evacuated The ISS Because Russia Wanted To Drill The Hull And Use A Saw To Fix A Leak - Jalopnik
item.description:
1. NASA Almost Evacuated The ISS Because Russia Wanted To Drill The Hull And Use A Saw To Fix A Leak  Jalopnik

  4. NASA said nyet to Roscosmos plan to cut into leaky ISS segment  The Register

  4. Black hole winds may be robbing giant galaxies of their future stars  ScienceDaily

item.title: Dark energy is still accelerating the expansion of the universe, and astronomers are relieved. 'Thankfully, we have averted this crisis' - Space

item.description:

item.title: New analysis finds signs of life on Mars actually came from Earth - Yahoo
item.description:
1. New analysis finds signs of life on Mars actually came from Earth  Yahoo
  2. Mars Organic Geochemistry-Are We Alone, and Where Did We Come From?  astrobiology.com
  3. Mars life search gets boost as rover test distinguishes mirrored biosignature molecules  Phys.org
  4. Scientists believe a vast ocean once covered the landing site of the ExoMars Rosalind Franklin rover  Earth.com
  5. Mars wasn’t always the frozen desert we see today. The evidence now suggests it once had rivers, long-lived lakes, and possibly a northern ocean — for a stretch of time longer than complex animals have existed on Earth.  Space Daily

item.title: The ocean produces roughly half of Earth’s oxygen — not the rainforest, which uses up most of what it makes — through photosynthesis by phytoplankton, microscopic marine organisms so abundant that a single teaspoon of seawater can contain as many as - Space Daily
item.description: The ocean produces roughly half of Earth’s oxygen — not the rainforest, which uses up most of what it makes — through photosynthesis by phytoplankton, microscopic marine organisms so abundant that a single teaspoon of seawater can contain as many as  Space Daily

item.title: When JWST looked at XLSSC 122, a galaxy cluster 10.4 billion light-years away, it caught gravity bending light from galaxies about 12 billion years in the past, and a dark matter core packed too tightly for cosmic noon - Space Daily
item.description:
1. When JWST looked at XLSSC 122, a galaxy cluster 10.4 billion light-years away, it caught gravity bending light from galaxies about 12 billion years in the past, and a dark matter core packed too tightly for cosmic noon  Space Daily

item.title: For as much as 14,000 years, the aspen clone called Pando has spread across 106 Utah acres as roughly 47,000 trunks, 13 million pounds of wood and roots, and one male organism still trying to replace itself through shoots the deer keep eating - Space Daily
item.description: For as much as 14,000 years, the aspen clone called Pando has spread across 106 Utah acres as roughly 47,000 trunks, 13 million pounds of wood and roots, and one male organism still trying to replace itself through shoots the deer keep eating  Space Daily

item.title: In 1946, a captured Nazi V-2 rocket launched from White Sands carried a 35mm motion picture camera to an altitude of 105 kilometres, and the grainy black-and-white frames it brought back became the first photographs of Earth taken from space — years befo - Space Daily
item.description: In 1946, a captured Nazi V-2 rocket launched from White Sands carried a 35mm motion picture camera to an altitude of 105 kilometres, and the grainy black-and-white frames it brought back became the first photographs of Earth taken from space — years befo  Space Daily

item.title: Musk's original 2001 Mars plan was almost childishly simple: land a small greenhouse on the red planet to reignite public excitement for space, the seed idea that grew into SpaceX - Space Daily
item.description: Musk's original 2001 Mars plan was almost childishly simple: land a small greenhouse on the red planet to reignite public excitement for space, the seed idea that grew into SpaceX  Space Daily

item.title: During the 1859 solar storm, telegraph operators disconnected their batteries entirely — yet messages kept flowing on the storm's own current as sparks showered from the machines and the paper caught fire - Space Daily

item.title: America's only landing on Venus was an accident — in 1978 NASA dropped four cone-shaped probes into the Venusian atmosphere, none built to survive hitting the ground, and one of them struck the surface, kept transmitting for 67 minutes and 37 seconds, - 19FortyFive

item.title: No spacecraft has ever landed in the outer solar system — except one: the Huygens probe, which parachuted through Titan's orange haze in 2005 and touched down more than a billion kilometres from Earth in cold that dropped below minus 170 degrees Cels - Space Daily
item.description: No spacecraft has ever landed in the outer solar system — except one: the Huygens probe, which parachuted through Titan's orange haze in 2005 and touched down more than a billion kilometres from Earth in cold that dropped below minus 170 degrees Cels  Space Daily

item.title: The notebooks Marie Curie used in her research remain radioactively contaminated more than a hundred years later. Some Curie papers have been found contaminated with radium-226, an isotope with a half-life of about 1,600 years, and original volumes ar - Space Daily

item.title: In December 2024 a NASA spacecraft flew straight into the Sun's outer atmosphere at 430,000 miles per hour, the fastest any human-made object has ever moved, through a region millions of degrees hot — and survived because a 4.5-inch carbon shield kept - 19FortyFive
item.description:
1. In December 2024 a NASA spacecraft flew straight into the Sun's outer atmosphere at 430,000 miles per hour, the fastest any human-made object has ever moved, through a region millions of degrees hot — and survived because a 4.5-inch carbon shield kept  19FortyFive
  2. NASA's Parker Solar Probe flew in and out of the solar corona and found a source of high-energy particles that no existing model had predicted  Space Daily
  3. A spacecraft travelling at Parker Solar Probe’s peak speed would reach the Moon in about 35 minutes — a trip that took Apollo 11 roughly three days  Space Daily
